request.question: "Scheherazade", composed by Nikolai Rimsky-Korasakov is known as this type of music?

title: Russian Easter Festival Overture
Glenn Schellenberg
Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov
Cinderella (Prokofiev)
Christmas Eve (opera)
Zov Djungley
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky and The Five
Facciolella castlei
Arturo Berutti
Scheherazade (Rimsky-Korsakov)
sentences: Russian Easter Festival Overture: Overture on Liturgical Themes (Russian: Светлый праздник, "Svetliy prazdnik" ), Op.
36, also known as the Great Russian Easter Overture, is a concert overture written by the Russian composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov between August 1887 and April 1888.
It was dedicated to the memories of Modest Mussorgsky and Alexander Borodin, two members of the group of composers known in English as "The Five".
It is the last of what many call his three most exceptionally brilliant orchestral works, preceded by "Capriccio Espagnol" and "Scheherazade".
The work received its premiere at a Russian symphony concert in St. Petersburg in late December 1888.
Glenn Schellenberg is a Canadian composer.
He is a frequent collaborator of director John Greyson, having composed the music for three of Greyson's films.
For one of these films, "Zero Patience", Schellenberg was nominated, along with Greyson, for a Genie Award for Best Original Song for the song "Just Like Scheherazade".
Nikolai Andreyevich Rimsky-Korsakov (18 March [O.S. 6 March] 1844 21 June [O.S. 8 June] 1908 ) was a Russian composer, and a member of the group of composers known as The Five.
He was a master of orchestration.

His best-known orchestral compositions—"Capriccio Espagnol", the "Russian Easter Festival Overture", and the symphonic suite "Scheherazade"—are staples of the classical music repertoire, along with suites and excerpts from some of his 15 operas.
"Scheherazade" is an example of his frequent use of fairy tale and folk subjects.
Cinderella (Russian: Золушка , "Zolushka"; French: "Cendrillon") Op. 87, is a ballet composed by Sergei Prokofiev to a scenario by Nikolai Volkov.
It is one of his most popular and melodious compositions, and has inspired a great many choreographers since its inception.
The piece was composed between 1940 and 1944.
Part way through writing it Prokofiev broke off to write his opera "War and Peace".
The premiere of "Cinderella" was conducted by Yuri Fayer on November 21, 1945 at the Bolshoi Theatre, with choreography by Rostislav Zakharov and Galina Ulanova in the title role.
"Cinderella" is notable for its jubilant music, lush scenery, and for the comic double-roles of the step-sisters (which can be performed in travesti), more mad than bad in this treatment.
Christmas Eve (Russian: Ночь перед Рождеством , "Noch' pered Rozhdestvom"), is an opera in four acts with music and libretto by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov.
Composed between 1894 and 1895, Rimsky-Korsakov based his opera on a short story, "Christmas Eve", from Nikolai Gogol's "Evenings on a Farm Near Dikanka".
The story had been used as the basis for an opera at least three times previously, including for Tchaikovsky's "Vakula the Smith" (1874).
Oliver Knussen writes that "Rimsky is only interested in recreating the atmosphere of the folk-tale, fleshing it out for his stage pageant in a comparable way to Humperdinck in "Hansel".
Zov Djungley (Russian: Зов джунглей , lit.
"Call of Jungles") was a TEFI-awarded Russian children's game show created and hosted by Sergei Suponev.
Was aired on Saturdays for two years from 1993 on Channel One.
In 1995 it was brought back to be hosted by Pyotr Fyodorov Sr. for 1997 and 1998, and Nikolai Gadomsky between 1998 and 2002.
Music for the show was composed by Viktor Prudkovsky and Sergei Suponev performed the opening theme music "Djungli Zovut" (Russian: Джунгли зовут , lit.
"Jungles Are Calling").
In mid- to late-19th-century Russia, Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky and a group of composers known as The Five had differing opinions as to whether should be composed following Western or native practices.

Tchaikovsky wanted to write professional compositions of such quality that they would stand up to Western scrutiny and thus transcend national barriers, yet remain distinctively Russian in melody, rhythm and other compositional characteristics.

The Five, made up of composers Mily Balakirev, Alexander Borodin, César Cui, Modest Mussorgsky, and Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov, sought to produce a specifically Russian kind of art music, rather than one that imitated older European music or relied on European-style conservatory training.
While Tchaikovsky himself used folk songs in some of his works, for the most part he tried to follow Western practices of composition, especially in terms of tonality and tonal progression.
Also, unlike Tchaikovsky, none of The Five were academically trained in composition; in fact, their leader, Balakirev, considered academicism a threat to musical imagination.

Along with critic Vladimir Stasov, who supported The Five, Balakirev attacked relentlessly both the Saint Petersburg Conservatory, from which Tchaikovsky had graduated, and its founder Anton Rubinstein, orally and in print.
Facciolella castlei is an eel in the family Nettastomatidae (duckbill/witch eels).
It was described by Nikolai Vasilyevich Parin and Emma Stanislavovna Karmovskaya in 1985.
It is a marine, deep water-dwelling eel which is known from Chile, in the southeastern Pacific Ocean.
The type specimen was retrieved from a depth of 230 metres.
Arturo Berutti (c. 27 March 1862 in San Juan, Argentina - 3 January 1938 in Buenos Aires) was an Argentinian composer of classical music and librettos.
He was best known for his notable theme "Pampa" (1897).
The opera was based on the life of Juan Moreira.
One of the influential Argentinian opera composers of the late 19th and early 20th century and his music was influenced by the Italian opera.
In 1895, he composed the opera "Taras Bulba" inspired on the novel by Nikolai Gogol.
Scheherazade, also commonly Sheherazade (Russian: Шехераза́да , "Shekherazáda"; ] ), Op.
35, is a symphonic suite composed by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov in 1888 and based on "One Thousand and One Nights", also known as "The Arabian Nights".

This orchestral work combines two features typical of Russian music and of Rimsky-Korsakov in particular: dazzling, colorful orchestration and an interest in the East, which figured greatly in the history of Imperial Russia, as well as orientalism in general.
The name "Scheherazade" refers to the main character "Shahrazad" of the "One Thousand and One Nights".
It is considered Rimsky-Korsakov's most popular work.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov | Nikolai Andreyevich Rimsky-Korsakov (18 March [O.S. 6 March] 1844 21 June [O.S. 8 June] 1908 ) was a Russian composer, and a member of the group of composers known as The Five.  He was a master of orchestration.  His best-known orchestral compositions—"Capriccio Espagnol", the "Russian Easter Festival Overture", and the symphonic suite "Scheherazade"—are staples of the classical music repertoire, along with suites and excerpts from some of his 15 operas.  "Scheherazade" is an example of his frequent use of fairy tale and folk subjects.

Scheherazade (Rimsky-Korsakov) | Scheherazade, also commonly Sheherazade (Russian: Шехераза́да , "Shekherazáda"; ] ), Op.  35, is a symphonic suite composed by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov in 1888 and based on "One Thousand and One Nights", also known as "The Arabian Nights".  This orchestral work combines two features typical of Russian music and of Rimsky-Korsakov in particular: dazzling, colorful orchestration and an interest in the East, which figured greatly in the history of Imperial Russia, as well as orientalism in general.  The name "Scheherazade" refers to the main character "Shahrazad" of the "One Thousand and One Nights".  It is considered Rimsky-Korsakov's most popular work.

Song of Scheherazade | Song of Scheherazade is a 1947 American musical film directed by Walter Reisch.  It tells the story of an imaginary episode in the life of the Russian composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov (Jean-Pierre Aumont), in 1865, when he was a young naval officer on shore leave in Morocco.  It also features Yvonne De Carlo as a Spanish dancer named Cara de Talavera, Eve Arden as her mother, and Brian Donlevy as the ship's captain.  Charles Kullman (credited as Charles Kullmann), a tenor with the Metropolitan Opera, plays the ship's doctor, Klin, who sings two of Rimsky-Korsakov's melodies.

Scheherazade and Other Stories | Scheherazade and Other Stories is the sixth studio album by the British progressive rock band Renaissance, released in 1975.  Some critics consider it their best album, although others prefer earlier albums.  This is the first album in which Renaissance (Annie Haslam's version) did not use quotes from actual classical pieces.  Contrary to popular belief, "Song of Scheherazade" is not based on Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov's "Scheherazade", but does have a recurring six-note motif that alludes to that work.

Rimsky-Korsakov Monument | The Rimsky-Korsakov monument is a bronze statue of Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov (1844-1908), the Russian composer, teacher, conductor, public figure, music critic, and member of the "Mighty Handful".

Russian Easter Festival Overture | Russian Easter Festival Overture: Overture on Liturgical Themes (Russian: Светлый праздник, "Svetliy prazdnik" ), Op.  36, also known as the Great Russian Easter Overture, is a concert overture written by the Russian composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov between August 1887 and April 1888.  It was dedicated to the memories of Modest Mussorgsky and Alexander Borodin, two members of the group of composers known in English as "The Five".  It is the last of what many call his three most exceptionally brilliant orchestral works, preceded by "Capriccio Espagnol" and "Scheherazade".  The work received its premiere at a Russian symphony concert in St. Petersburg in late December 1888.
List of compositions by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov | The following is a working list of compositions by the composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov (born 1844).
hop1_titles: List of compositions by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov
response.summary_1: Passages identify Rimsky-Korsakov's Scheherazade (Op. 35) as a symphonic/orchestral work: e.g., Passage 2 states "'Scheherazade... Op. 35, is a symphonic suite'"; Passage 1 refers to the "symphonic suite 'Scheherazade'"; Passage 2 also calls it "This orchestral work". No additional retrieval is needed—the provided texts directly support the work being a symphonic suite / orchestral work.
response.hop2_query: Rimsky-Korsakov Scheherazade Op 35 symphonic suite orchestral work
hop2_docs: Scheherazade (Rimsky-Korsakov) | Scheherazade, also commonly Sheherazade (Russian: Шехераза́да , "Shekherazáda"; ] ), Op.  35, is a symphonic suite composed by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov in 1888 and based on "One Thousand and One Nights", also known as "The Arabian Nights".  This orchestral work combines two features typical of Russian music and of Rimsky-Korsakov in particular: dazzling, colorful orchestration and an interest in the East, which figured greatly in the history of Imperial Russia, as well as orientalism in general.  The name "Scheherazade" refers to the main character "Shahrazad" of the "One Thousand and One Nights".  It is considered Rimsky-Korsakov's most popular work.

Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov | Nikolai Andreyevich Rimsky-Korsakov (18 March [O.S. 6 March] 1844 21 June [O.S. 8 June] 1908 ) was a Russian composer, and a member of the group of composers known as The Five.  He was a master of orchestration.  His best-known orchestral compositions—"Capriccio Espagnol", the "Russian Easter Festival Overture", and the symphonic suite "Scheherazade"—are staples of the classical music repertoire, along with suites and excerpts from some of his 15 operas.  "Scheherazade" is an example of his frequent use of fairy tale and folk subjects.

Capriccio Espagnol | Capriccio espagnol, Op.  34, is the common Western title for a five movement orchestral suite, based on Spanish folk melodies, composed by the Russian composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov in 1887.  Rimsky-Korsakov originally intended to write the work for a solo violin with orchestra, but later decided that a purely orchestral work would do better justice to the lively melodies.  The Russian title is Каприччио на испанские темы (literally, Capriccio on Spanish Themes).

Russian Symphony Concerts | The idea for the Russian Symphony Concerts was Rimsky-Korsakov's.  He had become acquainted with Belyayev at the weekly "quartet Fridays" ("Les Vendredis") held at Belayev's home.  Belayev had already taken a keen interest in the musical future of the teenage Alexander Glazunov, who had been one of Rimsky-Korsakov's composition students.  In 1884, Belayev rented out a hall and hired an orchestra to play Glazunov's First Symphony plus an orchestral suite Glazunov had just composed.  Glazunov was to conduct part of this concert.  Seeing he was not ready to do this, Rimsky-Korsakov volunteered to take his place.  This "rehearsal," as Rimsky-Korsakov called it, went well and pleased both Belayev and the invited audience.  Buoyed by the success of the rehearsal, Belayev decided the following season to give a public concert of works by Glazunov and other composers.  Rimsky-Korsakov's piano concerto was played, along with Glazunov's symphonic poem "Stenka Razin".

Antar (Rimsky-Korsakov) | Antar is a composition for symphony orchestra in four movements by the Russian composer Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov.  He wrote the piece in 1868 but revised it in 1875 and 1891.  He initially called the work his Second Symphony.  He later reconsidered and called it a symphonic suite.  It was first performed in 1869 at a concert of the Russian Musical Society.

Les Sylphides | Les Sylphides (] ) is a short, non-narrative "ballet blanc".  Its original choreography was by Michel Fokine, with music by Frédéric Chopin orchestrated by Alexander Glazunov.  Glazunov had already set some of the music in 1892 as a purely orchestral suite, under the title Chopiniana, Op. 46.  In that form it was introduced to the public in December 1893, conducted by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov.

Symphony No. 1 (Rimsky-Korsakov) | Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov composed his Symphony No. 1 in E minor, Op.  1 (originally in E-flat minor), between 1861 and 1865 under the guidance of Mily Balakirev.  Balakirev also premiered the work at a concert of the Free Music School in December 1865.  Rimsky-Korsakov revised the work in 1884.
response.summary_2: Scheherazade, Op. 35 by Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov is a symphonic suite — an orchestral work. Passage 1 explicitly describes it as "a symphonic suite" (see Passage 1).
